coredata.dc:title: CuS@CDs nanozymes with photothermal-enhanced peroxidase-like activities for colorimetric/fluorescent detection of glutathione and ferric ions in the coexisting system
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Huan
Zhao, Lijia
Gao, Zhihua
Cui, Anni
Mu, Xin
Wang, Xiaolu
Shan, Guiye
Chen, Yanwei
coredata.dc:description: Abnormal levels of glutathione (GSH) and ferric ions (Fe3+) can break the redox balance in the human body and lead to neurodegenerative diseases. However, it is still challenging to detect Fe3+ and GSH simultaneously in the coexisting system. Herein, a colorimetric/fluorescent sensing platform based on copper sulfide @ carbon dots nanocomposites (CuS@CDs NCs) is constructed for simultaneously detecting GSH and Fe3+ in the coexisting system. CuS@CDs NCs show photothermal effect, leading to the improvement of peroxidase-like activity of CuS@CDs NCs for colorimetric analysis of GSH. Moreover, Fe3+ can be detected through the fluorescence quenching of CuS@CDs NCs based on the inner filter effect (IFE). The results show the detection limits toward GSH and Fe3+ as low as 0.0527 μM and 0.0163 μM, respectively. In order to realize the detection of GSH and Fe3+ in the coexisting system, hydrogen peroxide is added into the system to reduce the mutual interference between GSH and Fe3+. The sensing strategy reaches wider linear GSH detection range of 0–500 μM in the coexisting system. The serum samples are successfully analyzed, suggesting the feasibility of sensing strategy. This sensing platform provides a new pathway for multicomponent biodetection in the coexisting system.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Copper sulfide
Carbon dots
Peroxidase-like activity
Fluorescence property
Glutathione
Ferric ions

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Peroxidase-like activity and kinetic analysis of CuS@CDs NCs
Detection of GSH and Fe3+ in the coexisting system
Fluorescent detection of Fe3+ based on fluorescence characterization of CuS@CDs NCs
Colorimetric detection of GSH based on photothermal-enhanced peroxidase-like activity of CuS@CDs NCs
Selectivity of CuS@CDs NCs towards GSH and Fe3+
Colorimetric/fluorescent detection of GSH and Fe3+ in the coexisting system
Detection of GSH and Fe3+ in serum samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 11774048 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The synthesis of CuS@CDs NCs; (b) TEM image of CuS NPs. Inset: HRTEM image of CuS NPs; (c) TEM image of CDs. Inset: HRTEM image of CDs; (d) and (e) TEM images of CuS@CDs NCs. Inset: HRTEM image of CuS@CDs NCs.

(a) Zeta potentials of CDs, CuS NPs and CuS@CDs NCs; (b) FT-IR spectra of CDs, CuS NPs and CuS@CDs NCs; (c) XPS characterization of survey scan spectra of CDs and CuS@CDs NCs; (d) XPS characterizations of Cu 2p of CuS NPs and CuS@CDs NCs; (e) XRD patterns of CDs, CuS NPs and CuS@CDs NCs; (f) UV–vis absorbance spectra of CDs, CuS NPs and CuS@CDs NCs.

(a) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CDs, CuS NPs and CuS@CDs NCs; (b) UV–vis absorbance spectrum of Fe3+, fluorescence excitation spectrum of CuS@CDs NCs and fluorescence emission spectra of CuS@CDs NCs and CuS@CDs NCs+Fe3+; (c) Fluorescence decay of CuS@CDs NCs and CuS@CDs NCs+Fe3+; (d) Fluorescence spectra of CuS@CDs NCs with different concentrations of Fe3+ and the relationship between fluorescence intensity at 425 nm and Fe3+ concentrations. Inset: The linear calibration plot for Fe3+ detection. The error bars indicate calculated standard deviations from several sets of independent measurements (n = 3).

(a) The UV–vis absorbance spectra of CDs+TMB+H2O2, CuS NPs+TMB+H2O2 and CuS@CDs NCs+TMB+H2O2. Inset: Photographs of colorimetric systems incubated with (I) CDs, (Ⅱ) CuS NPs and (Ⅲ) CuS@CDs NCs; (b) Photothermal effect of CDs, CuS NPs and CuS@CDs NCs. Inset: Infrared thermographies of CuS@CDs NCs solution with 808 nm laser irradiation; Lineweaver-Burk double reciprocal plots of CuS@CDs NCs and CuS@CDs NCs-NIR corresponding to (c) TMB and (d) H2O2; (e) ESR spectra of H2O2, CDs+H2O2, CuS NPs+TMB+H2O2, CuS@CDs NCs +H2O2, and CuS@CDs NCs +H2O2-NIR in the presence of DMPO; (f) UV–vis absorption spectra of oxTMB with different GSH concentrations and dependence curves of absorbance at 652 nm with different concentrations of GSH. Inset: The linear calibration plot for GSH detection. The error bars indicate calculated standard deviations from several sets of independent measurements (n = 3).

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of oxTMB with different interferents in the presence of CuS@CDs NCs, TMB and H2O2; (b) Selectivity of CuS@CDs NCs towards GSH based on colorimetric assay; (c) UV–vis absorbance spectra of different cations and fluorescence emission spectrum of CuS@CDs NCs; (d) Selectivity of CuS@CDs NCs towards Fe3+ based on fluorescent assay. The error bars indicate calculated standard deviations from several sets of independent measurements (n = 3).

(a) The UV–vis absorbance spectra of GSH+DTNB, GSH+Fe3++DTNB and GSH+Fe3++H2O2 +DTNB based on DTNB as the trapping agent; (b) The fluorescent spectra of CuS@CDs NCs+Fe3+, CuS@CDs NCs+Fe3++GSH and CuS@CDs NCs+Fe3++GSH+H2O2; (c) UV–vis absorbance spectra of oxTMB with various GSH concentrations in the coexisting system and the relationship between absorbance of oxTMB at 652 nm and GSH concentrations; (d) Fluorescence spectra of CuS@CDs NCs with different concentrations of Fe3+ in the coexisting system and the relationship between fluorescence intensity of CuS@CDs NCs at 425 nm and Fe3+ concentrations. The error bars indicate calculated standard deviations from several sets of independent measurements (n = 3).
GSH and Fe3+ detection with human serum samples.

head.ce:title: CuS@CDs nanozymes with photothermal-enhanced peroxidase-like activities for colorimetric/fluorescent detection of glutathione and ferric ions in the coexisting system

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre for Advanced Optoelectronic Functional Materials Research, Key Laboratory for UV Light-Emitting Materials and Technology of the Ministry of Education, Northeast Normal University, Changchun, Jilin 130024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Centre for Advanced Optoelectronic Functional Materials Research, Key Laboratory for UV Light-Emitting Materials and Technology of the Ministry of Education, Northeast Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Centre for Advanced Optoelectronic Functional Materials Research, Key Laboratory for UV Light-Emitting Materials and Technology of the Ministry of Education, Northeast Normal University, Changchun, Jilin, 130024, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Abnormal levels of glutathione (GSH) and ferric ions (Fe3+) can break the redox balance in the human body and lead to neurodegenerative diseases. However, it is still challenging to detect Fe3+ and GSH simultaneously in the coexisting system. Herein, a colorimetric/fluorescent sensing platform based on copper sulfide @ carbon dots nanocomposites (CuS@CDs NCs) is constructed for simultaneously detecting GSH and Fe3+ in the coexisting system. CuS@CDs NCs show photothermal effect, leading to the improvement of peroxidase-like activity of CuS@CDs NCs for colorimetric analysis of GSH. Moreover, Fe3+ can be detected through the fluorescence quenching of CuS@CDs NCs based on the inner filter effect (IFE). The results show the detection limits toward GSH and Fe3+ as low as 0.0527 μM and 0.0163 μM, respectively. In order to realize the detection of GSH and Fe3+ in the coexisting system, hydrogen peroxide is added into the system to reduce the mutual interference between GSH and Fe3+. The sensing strategy reaches wider linear GSH detection range of 0–500 μM in the coexisting system. The serum samples are successfully analyzed, suggesting the feasibility of sensing strategy. This sensing platform provides a new pathway for multicomponent biodetection in the coexisting system.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Reactive oxygen species (ROS) play important roles in many physiological processes [1,2]. Excess ROS may cause DNA damage, protein oxidation and ferroptosis, leading to various diseases such as Parkinson's disease、Alzheimer's disease and Huntington's disease [3,4]. Fe3+, as cofactor with oxidizability, can trigger the generation of ROS. GSH, as an antioxidant, can effectively eliminate ROS. The proper level of GSH and Fe3+ is essential in maintaining the ROS balance in the body [5]. Therefore, it is of great significant to develop an effective detection method to monitor GSH and Fe3+ levels in the coexisting system simultaneously. However, traditional methods cannot realize the detection of GSH and Fe3+ in the coexisting system, which can only detect stepwise through fluorescence spectrophotometry [6], electrochemistry [7] and colorimetry [8]. Hence, the construction of sensing strategy for simultaneous detection of Fe3+ and GSH in the coexisting system is urgently needed.

Multifunctional nanozymes have received much attention due to the ability to integrate multiple properties into one nanoplatform [9]. Copper sulfide nanoparticles (CuS NPs) as Cu-based nanozymes have attracted much concerned in biochemical analysis due to the peroxidase-like activity [10]. Meanwhile, CuS NPs exhibit excellent photothermal conversion performance, leading to the enhancement of peroxidase-like activity for biomolecules recognition via enzymatic colorimetry [11]. Carbon dots (CDs) as fluorescence nanomaterials have abundant surface functional groups, which is prone to combine with other functional nanomaterials to construct nanocomposites [12]. The fluorescence intensity of CDs can be affected by metal ions and further achieve the effective metal ions detection via aggregation, IFE and so on [13]. Hence, the construction of multifunctional sensor based on CuS and CDs is promising for sensitive detection of GSH and Fe3+, simultaneously.

In this work, CuS@CDs NCs with photothermal-enhanced peroxidase-like activity and fluorescence property were fabricated for colorimetric/fluorescent GSH and Fe3+ detection simultaneously. Peroxidase-like activity of CuS@CDs NCs can be enhanced by photothermal effect, achieving colorimetric detection of GSH. The fluorescence of CuS@CDs NCs is modulated by Fe3+ due to IFE, which can realize the fluorescent detection of Fe3+. Moreover, hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is introduced into the coexisting system for GSH and Fe3+ detection by decreasing the effect of GSH on Fe3+. This study provides new perspectives for designing multifunctional nanozymes for accurate quantitative GSH and Fe3+ detection, simultaneously.

Urea, sodium citrate dihydrate (C6H5Na3O7 ·2 H2O) and p-phenylenediamine were supplied by Beijing Chemical Reagent Company. Sodium sulfide nonahydrate (Na2S·9 H2O) was brought from Xilong Chemical Co., Ltd. Copper chloride (CuCl2) was brought from Tianjin Fuchen Chemical Reagent Factory. 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), GSH and 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) were supplied by Rhawn.
Apparatuses are listed in the Electronic Supplementary Material.

The solution (30 mL) containing p-phenylenediamine (0.12 g) and urea (0.12 g) was heated in the autoclave at 160 °C for 10 h to prepare CDs [14]. Then, CDs solution was filtered via membrane filter and dialysis bag. CuS NPs were synthesized with slight modifications [15]. CuCl2 (17 mg) and C6H5Na3O7 ·2 H2O (20 mg) were dispersed in the deionized water (90 mL). The solution was mixed with Na2S·9 H2O (2 mg/mL, 10 mL) and stirred at 90 °C for 15 min to obtain CuS NPs. Finally, 3 mL of CuS NPs was mixed with CDs (500 μL) under room temperature and the mixture was shaken overnight.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Peroxidase-like activity and kinetic analysis of CuS@CDs NCs
ce:section.ce:para: The peroxidase-like activity of CuS@CDs NCs was evaluated by monitoring the absorption spectra of 2 mL colorimetric system (pH=4) containing H2O2 (22 μL, 1 M), TMB (320 μL, 5 mM) and CuS@CDs NCs (0.4 mg/mL) after incubation for 20 min

Steady-state kinetic analysis was studied. H2O2 (10 mM) was used as substrate and the concentrations of TMB were changed. Subsequently, the concentration of TMB (0.8 mM) was controlled and the concentrations of H2O2 were changed from 5 mM to 12 mM. 1/ν = Km/Vmax ·(1/[S]+1/Km) was utilized to calculate the steady-state kinetic parameters.

H2O2 (22 μL, 1 M), TMB (320 μL, 5 mM) and CuS@CDs NCs (0.4 mg/mL) were incubated in the buffer solution for 20 min with near-infrared (NIR) light irradiation (1.5 W/cm2) for 17 min. Subsequently, GSH (0.5 mL) with different concentrations from 5 to 500 μM were mixed in the CuS@CDs NCs/H2O2/TMB colorimetric system, respectively. Then, the absorption spectra of oxidized TMB (oxTMB) were measured.

0.5 mL different concentrations of Fe3+ from 5 to 5000 μM were added into the 2 mL CuS@CDs NCs (0.4 mg/mL). The fluorescence spectra of CuS@CDs NCs solution containing Fe3+ were recorded at 325 nm excitation wavelength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of GSH and Fe3+ in the coexisting system
ce:section.ce:para: H2O2 (20 μL, 0.3 M) was added to the coexisting system containing GSH and Fe3+. After 20 min, 2 mL of CuS@CDs NCs (0.4 mg/mL) was added into the mixed solution and fluorescence spectrum of CuS@CDs NCs was recorded for detecting Fe3+. Subsequently, TMB (320 μL, 5 mM) was used as chromogenic agent and GSH concentration was detected by monitoring the absorbance spectrum of oxTMB.

The synthetic process of CuS@CDs NCs is shown in Fig. 1a. CuS NPs were synthesized by chemical bath deposition method, while CDs were prepared through hydrothermal method. The morphologies of CuS NPs, CDs and CuS@CDs NCs were investigated via transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images. Fig. 1b and S1a exhibit CuS NPs are spherical with the size about 15 nm. TEM image show pure CDs are ultrafine particles with good dispersion and the diameter of CDs is about 2.5 nm (Fig. 1c and S1b). As shown in Fig. 1d, the CuS@CDs NCs exhibit orderly chain-like connections. Fig. 1e shows that CuS@CDs NCs exhibit core-shell nanostructure. It can be observed that the typical lattice fringe spacing of CuS@CDs NCs with d= 0.312 nm at edge is attributed to (002) plane of CDs and d= 0.281 nm at core is assigned to (103) plane of CuS NPs. The results confirm that the CDs shells are formed on the surface of CuS NPs.

The formation process of CuS@CDs NCs was further investigated. As shown in Fig. 2a, zeta potentials of CuS NPs, CDs and CuS@CDs NCs were measured. CuS NPs and CDs show opposite potentials, indicating the occurrence of electrostatic interaction between negative charges of CuS NPs and positive charges of CDs during the synthesis of CuS@CDs NCs. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy is utilized to investigate the surface chemical groups of CuS@CDs NCs (Fig. 2b). The peak at 3240 cm−1 is assigned to the N-H stretching vibration [16]. Moreover, the peak of C-N at 1209 cm−1 is related to the acylamide of CuS@CDs NCs, suggesting the successful cross-linking reaction between carboxyl groups and amino groups. The cross-linking reaction further promoted the formation of carbon shells on the surface of CuS NPs. Hence, formation process of chain-like CuS@CDs NCs was determined.

Subsequently, the survey scan spectra of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) display the existence of O, N, C, Cu and S elements (Fig. 2c). The Cu 2p shows double peaks of Cu 2p3/2 (931.18 eV) and Cu 2p1/2 (951.08 eV) (Fig. 2d) [17]. The Cu 2p peaks of CuS@CDs NCs show a negative shift, which suggests the electronic interaction between CuS NPs and CDs [18]. The structure information is reflected by X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns (Fig. 2e). A broader hump of CuS@CDs NCs at 28.6° is verified to be (200) crystallographic plane of CDs. The diffraction peaks at 31.6°, 56.0° and 66.3° are attributed to (103), (200) and (206) crystal planes of the covellite CuS NPs. The absorption spectra of CuS NPs, CDs and CuS@CDs NCs were further investigated. CDs present two main absorption peaks at 277 nm and 346 nm, ascribed to π-π* transition of CN and n-π* transition of CO bonds, respectively (Fig. 2f) [14]. CuS NPs exhibit the absorption peak at around 980 nm. The UV–vis absorbance spectrum of CuS@CDs NCs is similar to CuS NPs in the NIR region, suggesting that CuS@CDs NCs exhibit potential for good photothermal conversion ability. The absorption region of CuS@CDs NCs at 200–400 nm is related to the exciton-plasmon coupling, which indicates the interaction between CuS NPs and CDs [19].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent detection of Fe3+ based on fluorescence characterization of CuS@CDs NCs
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence performance of CuS@CDs NCs was further investigated. CuS@CDs NCs exhibit fluorescence emission peak at 425 nm under the excitation wavelength of 325 nm, suggesting that CuS@CDs NCs retain the fluorescence performance of CDs ( Fig. 3a). The fluorescence intensity of CuS@CDs NCs is partially decreased compared with CDs. As shown in Fig. 3b, the fluorescence of CuS@CDs NCs can be quenched by Fe3+. Hence, the possible quenching mechanism of Fe3+ towards CuS@CDs NCs was analyzed. The absorbance spectrum of Fe3+ overlaps well with the fluorescence excitation spectrum and partially with the excitation spectrum of CuS@CDs NCs, suggesting that the fluorescence quenching may be due to the IFE or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). The fluorescence lifetime of CuS@CDs NCs is 9.27 ns. Meanwhile, the fluorescence lifetime of CuS@CDs NCs is negligibly changed after combining with Fe3+, indicating that the quenching mechanism cannot be dynamic quenching of FRET (Fig. 3c). Therefore, the results demonstrate that the fluorescence quenching of CuS@CDs NCs by Fe3+ is attributed to IFE, which belongs to static quenching [20].

The fluorescence of CuS@CDs NCs can be inhibited by Fe3+ owing to IFE. Hence, the abilities of CuS@CDs NCs to detect Fe3+ were evaluated via fluorescent analysis. As shown in Fig. 3d, the fluorescence emission intensity of CuS@CDs NCs at 425 nm gradually decreases with the increase of Fe3+ concentration. The results show a good linear relationship in the Fe3+ concentration range from 5 to 100 μM. The limit of detection (LOD) of Fe3+ reaches 0.0163 μM. Subsequently, the fluorescence stability of CuS@CDs NCs was evaluated based on several factors. It can be observed that the fluorescence of CuS@CDs NCs remains stable over physiological pH values ranges (Fig. S2a). Fluorescence intensity of CuS@CDs NC exhibits negligible changes within the temperature range from 20° to 40°C (Fig. S2b). Moreover, CuS@CDs NCs show the excellent storage stability within 30 days (Fig. S2c). The above results suggest that CuS@CDs NCs show good fluorescence stability in the physiological condition. Furthermore, the stability of fluorescent detection performance towards Fe3+ is observed in different batches of CuS@CDs NCs (Fig. S3a). The results show that colorimetric fluorescence intensity of CuS@CDs NCs exhibit negligible difference, indicating that CuS@CDs NCs have good fluorescent detection stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric detection of GSH based on photothermal-enhanced peroxidase-like activity of CuS@CDs NCs
ce:section.ce:para: To realize the colorimetric detection of GSH, the peroxidase-like activity of CuS@CDs NCs was evaluated. CuS@CDs NCs with peroxidase-like activity can catalyze the decomposition of H2O2 to generate hydroxyl radicals (·OH). TMB, as colorimetric probe, can be oxidized to blue oxTMB by ·OH. As shown in Fig. 4a, CDs display no characteristic absorption peak of oxTMB at 652 nm in the presence of H2O2 and TMB, which proves that CDs have no peroxidase-like activity. The colorimetric system containing CuS NPs exhibits an absorption peak at 652 nm, suggesting that CuS NPs show the peroxidase-like activity. Compared with the CuS NPs+TMB+H2O2 group, CuS@CDs NCs+TMB+H2O2 group exhibits higher absorption peak. The results confirm CDs can enhance the peroxidase-like activity of CuS NPs. Subsequently, the photothermal behavior of CuS@CDs NCs was investigated to further determine the influence on the catalytic activity. As shown in Fig. 4b, CuS NPs and CuS@CDs NCs show good photothermal effect with 808 nm laser irradiation, while CDs have no photothermal conversion ability. The temperature variation of CuS@CDs NCs can be clearly observed through infrared thermal imaging. Fig. S4 shows the photothermal stability of CuS@CDs NCs in three cycles and the photothermal conversion efficiency (η) of CuS@CDs NCs is calculated to be 38.10%. Hence, the results prove that CuS@CDs NCs have excellent photothermal conversion ability.

The influence of photothermal effect on peroxidase-like activity of CuS@CDs NCs is further analyzed. Fig. S5 shows the peroxidase-like activity of CuS@CDs NCs is significantly improved with NIR light irradiation for 17 min. Michaelis-Menten curves were employed to study steady-state kinetics of CuS@CDs NCs with and without NIR light irradiation (Fig. S6). The kinetic parameters of maximum initial velocity (Vmax) and Michaelis constant (Km) are calculated via Lineweaver-Burk plots (Fig. 4c and d). Under the NIR light irradiation, the Vmax values for TMB and H2O2 are 13.10 × 10−8 M·s−1 and 16.52 × 10−8 M·s−1, respectively (Table S1). The Km values of TMB and H2O2 are 0.123 mM and 4.098 mM, respectively. Compared to the group without NIR irradiation, CuS@CDs NCs exhibit smaller Km values and faster Vmax values, indicating the superior peroxidase-like activity of CuS@CDs NCs due to photothermal effect.

The ·OH generated was determined in the catalytic process using electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra (Fig. 4e and S7). DMPO is used as radical trapping agent of ·OH. The DMPO/·OH adduct can be formed in the presence of ·OH, which shows the 1:2:2:1 quadruple ESR signal peaks. After incubating CuS NPs with DMPO and H2O2, four signal peaks with intensity radio of 1:2:2:1 are observed, suggesting that ·OH is generated. Compared to CuS NPs, ESR signal peaks intensity of CuS@CDs NCs is stronger. Therefore, CuS@CDs NCs can convert more H2O2 into ·OH than CuS NPs, which is attributed to the interfacial electron transfer between CuS and CDs (Fig. S8) [21]. The ESR signal intensity of CuS@CDs NCs is further enhanced with the NIR light irradiation. It suggests that NIR irradiation can promote more ·OH generation owing to the photothermal effect.

Furthermore, the effect of pH value and temperature on peroxidase-like activity was investigated (Fig. S9). The results show the optimal pH value for colorimetric assay is 4. The relative peroxidase-like activity of CuS@CDs NCs can maintain above 80% in the temperature range from 20° to 60°C, suggesting the good temperature stability of CuS@CDs NCs. Moreover, the peroxidase-like activity of CuS@CDs NCs demonstrates negligible changes after 30 days, indicating the excellent storage stability of CuS@CDs NCs. Meanwhile, the CuS@CDs NCs maintained good dissolution capacity after one year storage.

CuS@CDs NCs were utilized for colorimetric detection of GSH based on the peroxidase-like activity (Fig. S10). CuS@CDs NCs with peroxidase-like activity can catalyze H2O2 to generate ·OH, which further oxidize TMB to blue oxTMB. The oxTMB shows the absorption peak at 652 nm. GSH, as an antioxidant, can be detected by reducing the blue oxTMB to TMB, resulting in the decrease of oxTMB absorption intensity at 652 nm (Fig. 4f). Meanwhile, the color of colorimetric system changes from blue to colorless. The concentration of GSH displays a linear relationship versus the characteristic peak of oxTMB within the range of 5–300 μM. The LOD of GSH is 0.0527 μM. Moreover, it can be observed that colorimetric detection performance exhibits stability in different batches of CuS@CDs NCs (Fig. S3b). The results demonstrate that CuS@CDs NCs show excellent GSH detection performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of CuS@CDs NCs towards GSH and Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the CuS@CDs NCs toward GSH and Fe3+ was investigated. The absorbance of oxTMB was recorded after adding various amino acids (500 μM). As shown in Fig. 5a, compared with other inferences, the absorbance of oxTMB exhibits significant changes with the addition of GSH. Moreover, CuS@CDs NCs/TMB/H2O2 colorimetric system exhibits slight response signals to ascorbic acid (AA) and cysteine (Cys) due to the redox reactions (Fig. 5b). However, the biological concentrations of AA and Cys are much lower than GSH, indicating the negligible interference with GSH detection. Thus, the results reveal that CuS@CDs NCs show excellent selectivity to GSH. Furthermore, the selectivity of CuS@CDs NCs towards Fe3+ was studied. Several metal cations were selected as potential interferents. Fig. 5c shows the fluorescence emission band of CuS@CDs NCs overlaps with the absorption band of Fe3+, while no significant overlap is observed between CuS@CDs NCs and other metal cations. Hence, Fe3+ leads to the obvious changes in fluorescence intensity of CuS@CDs NCs, suggesting that CuS@CDs NCs exhibit the high selectivity to Fe3+ (Fig. 5d). Subsequently, the anti-interference ability of CuS@CDs NCs was evaluated (Fig. S11). It can be seen that interferents show negligible effect on colorimetric and fluorescent detection. Therefore, CuS@CDs NCs show excellent selectivity and anti-interference ability for GSH and Fe3+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric/fluorescent detection of GSH and Fe3+ in the coexisting system
ce:section.ce:para: Detection of GSH and Fe3+ in the coexisting system is challenging due to the redox reaction. However, the metabolic balance between GSH and Fe3+ can be reached through the regulation of H2O2 under physiological conditions. Hence, a sensing strategy is proposed for GSH and Fe3+ detection in the coexisting system through introducing H2O2. The interaction between GSH and Fe3+ in the coexisting system with and without H2O2 was analyzed. The presence of GSH is indicated by 5,5′-dithiobis-(2-nitrobenzoic acid) (DTNB), which exhibits the absorption peak at 412 nm after combining with GSH. The fluorescence of CuS@CDs NCs can be quenched by Fe3+ due to the IFE. Fe3+ in the coexisting system is analyzed through monitoring the fluorescence intensity of CuS@CDs NCs at 425 nm.

Firstly, the coexisting system of GSH and Fe3+ without H2O2 was studied. As shown in Fig. 6a, no absorbance peak at 412 nm is observed, suggesting GSH is consumed. Fig. 6b displays the fluorescence of CuS@CDs NCs is not quenched, which confirms the depletion of Fe3+. The results suggest that mutual interference exists between GSH and Fe3+ in the absence of H2O2, possibly due to the following redox reaction [22].
(1) Fe3+ + GSH = Fe2+ + GSSG

Secondly, in the presence of H2O2, the coexisting system of GSH and Fe3+ was further evaluated. It can be seen that the absorbance peak at 421 nm is not found and the fluorescence of CuS@CDs NCs at 425 nm is quenched. The results demonstrate that GSH is consumed, while Fe3+ is not affected in the coexisting system with H2O2. According to previous report, the reaction process may consist the following three reactions [23]:
(2) 2Fe3+ + GSH = 2Fe2+ + GSSG + 2 H+
(3) 2Fe2+ + H2O2 + 2 H+ = 2Fe3+ + 2 H2O
(4) GSH + H2O2 = GSSG + 2 H2O

Therefore, the level of GSH can be determined through monitoring the depletion of H2O2 in the coexisting system using TMB colorimetric method and the Fe3+ is detected via fluorescent method. As shown in Fig. 6c, with the increase of GSH, the absorbance of oxTMB at 652 nm decreases. A linear relationship between the absorption of oxTMB and GSH concentrations range from 0–500 μM is observed. The level of Fe3+ is detected by monitoring the fluorescence intensity of CuS@CDs NCs (Fig. 6d). The results display a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of CuS@CDs NCs and Fe3+ concentrations in the range of 5–100 μM. The fluorescent detection results of Fe3+ in the coexisting system are consistent with the results of detection individually, suggesting that GSH has negligible effect on Fe3+ in the coexisting system with H2O2. The above results demonstrate the feasibility of GSH and Fe3+ detection in the coexisting system. Subsequently, the selectivity of CuS@CDs NCs toward GSH and Fe3+ in the coexisting system was studied (Fig. S12). Compared with the coexisting system of GSH and Fe3+, the group containing interferents results in slight change of absorbance and fluorescence intensity. The results demonstrated that sensing strategy shows excellent selectivity for GSH and Fe3+ in the coexisting system. Hence, GSH and Fe3+ detection can be achieved in the coexisting system through introduction of H2O2, and the sensing strategy is significant for realizing real sample detection. Compared with other analysis methods, this method realizes the detection of GSH and Fe3+ in the coexisting system. Meanwhile, the sensing platform exhibits excellent detection ranges and sensitivity (Table S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of GSH and Fe3+ in serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: The concentrations of Fe3+ and GSH in the human serum were determined by standard addition method ( Table 1). Blood samples were centrifuged to obtain serum samples (5000 rpm, 8 min). According to published literature, Fe3+ was released from transferrin [24]. Then, H2O2 was added into the 2-fold diluted human serum samples (500 μL). After incubating for 20 min, CuS@CDs NCs (0.4 mg/mL) were further added to the coexisting system and the fluorescence intensity of CuS@CDs NCs at 425 nm was monitored for Fe3+ detection. Subsequently, TMB (0.8 mM) was used as chromogenic agent for analyzing the level of GSH through monitoring the absorbance intensity of oxTMB at 652 nm [25]. Serum samples from three subjects were detected, and the results confirm the credibility of the method for GSH and Fe3+ detection (Table S3).

In conclusion, multifunctional CuS@CDs NCs with peroxidase-like activity and fluorescence property were successfully designed for colorimetric/fluorescent detection of GSH and Fe3+. The dual-signal responses for GSH and Fe3+ were achieved due to the redox reaction and IFE, which induced the changes of oxTMB absorbance and fluorescence emission intensity of CuS@CDs NCs. Moreover, the peroxidase-like activity was enhanced due to the photothermal effect. As a result, the CuS@CDs NCs exhibited excellent detection ranges of 5–300 μM for GSH and 5–100 μM for Fe3+. Meanwhile, the LOD of GSH and Fe3+ were as low as 0.0527 μM and 0.0163 μM, respectively. Further, a sensing strategy was proposed for detecting GSH and Fe3+ in the coexisting system via H2O2 introduction and the real samples are successfully realized. The above results show that the sensor based on CuS@CDs NCs not only exhibits high accuracy and selectivity, but also provides a potential tool for multicomponent detection in coexisting complex system.

Huan Wang: Investigation, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation, Software. Lijia Zhao: Methodology, Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Zhihua Gao: Data curation, Supervision. Anni Cui: Investigation, Data curation. Xin Mu: Investigation. Xiaolu Wang: Data curation. Guiye Shan: Investigation, Supervision, Data curation, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Yanwei Chen: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (11774048).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2023.134726.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Huan Wang received her bachelor's degree in Northeast Normal University in 2018. She is currently a doctoral candidate majoring in material physics and chemistry in Northeast Normal University and her main research interest is the fabrication of functional nanomaterials and their application in biology.

Lijia Zhao received his master's degree in Jilin Jianzhu University in 2022. He is currently a doctoral candidate in Northeast Normal University. His main research interest is in fabrication of multifunctional hydrogel.

Zhihua Gao received her Ph.D. degree in Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Now, she is working as Lecturer at College of Physics, Northeast Normal University, Jilin Province, China. Her research interests focus on physical properties and biomedical applications of functional nanomaterials.

Anni Cui received her bachelor's degree in Changchun University of Technology in 2018. She is currently a doctoral candidate in Northeast Normal University. Her main research interest is in biological nanotechnology.

Xin Mu received his master's degree in Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2019. Now, he is currently a doctoral candidate in Northeast Normal University, Jilin Province, China. His research interests focus on functional nanomaterials and their application in biological field.

Xiaolu Wang received her bachelor's degree in Harbin Normal University in 2021. She is currently working toward the master’s degree in Northeast Normal University. Her research interests focus on fabrication of multifunctional nanozymes and their application in biodetection.

Guiye Shan received her Ph.D. in Jilin University in 2004. Now, she is working as Professor at College of Physics, Northeast Normal University, Jilin Province, China. Her research interests focus on the photothermal effect and photodynamic process of noble metal and their application in biology.

Yanwei Chen received her Ph.D. degree in Physical from Changchun Institute of Optics, Fine Mehcanics and Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Jilin, China. She is working as Associate Professorial Fellow at College of Physics, Northeast Normal University, Jilin Province, China. Her research interests include functional fluorescent nanomaterials and biomaterials.
